chat.author: Kathleen Moriarty
chat.text: I agree with Yaroslav, for vulnerabilities in drafts, you fix them. Implementations are updated along with the updated draft and in most cases, the implementations are not released until after a document becomes an RFC. It's not IETF outreach, the focus should be on the reviewed and secured protocols that reach RFC level.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Kathleen Moriarty said:

I agree with Yaroslav, for vulnerabilities in drafts, you fix them. Implementations are updated along with the updated draft and in most cases, the implementations are not released until after a document becomes an RFC. It's not IETF outreach, the focus should be on the reviewed and secured protocols that reach RFC level.

You probably missed my on-mic comment as well as the clarification message above. The point was to involve not only implementers but also researchers who can find vulnerabilities in the drafts by doing formal analysis.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: I tend to think that we need regulation in this space, but the shape of that regulation seems very specifically designed to maximize authoritarian-style control and surveillance.

chat.author: Nathanael Ritz
chat.text:
Sec. 5 and 6 (specifically 6.4) of paper covers draft specification: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/408219182_Intra-handshakefail_CVE-2026-33697_High-severity_CVE_in_Attested_TLS

Section 6.4 of the publication is an excellent proof of transitive binding properties for the TLS 1.3 key schedule, and I commend the authors for presenting it. It does not demonstrate a flaw in either of those 2 drafts claimed as vulnerable, however. If that was demonstrable, I imagine the symposium would have otherwise allowed the claim to be made directly in the camera ready publication. But this entire discussion is really deeply off-topic, I think.

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: if self-declaration is out, then who is allowed to declare someone's age?  there must be a point of authoritarian control there, to MT's point.

chat.author: Tom Newton
chat.text: Martin - how much of the authoritarian style is the nature of the legislators, how much is it the lack of any other option, and how much is it a lack of knowledge. I wonder what we can do to make it easier for legislation to be sensible

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Nathanael Ritz said:


Sec. 5 and 6 (specifically 6.4) of paper covers draft specification: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/408219182_Intra-handshakefail_CVE-2026-33697_High-severity_CVE_in_Attested_TLS

Section 6.4 of the publication is an excellent proof of transitive binding properties for the TLS 1.3 key schedule, and I commend the authors for presenting it. It does not demonstrate a flaw in either of those 2 drafts claimed as vulnerable, however. If that was demonstrable, I imagine the symposium would have otherwise allowed the claim to be made directly in the camera ready publication. But this entire discussion is really deeply off-topic, I think.

It is not clear to me: Are you denying the vulnerabilities or are you denying the outreach to researchers doing formal analysis? My point here is the latter with the former as examplary.

chat.text:

Martin - how much of the authoritarian style is the nature of the legislators, how much is it the lack of any other option, and how much is it a lack of knowledge. I wonder what we can do to make it easier for legislation to be sensible

chat.author: Eliot Lear
chat.text: I mentioned this elsewhere.  A little bit of IETF history.  When @home formed in 1998, Milo Medin brought William Randolph Hearst to an IETF in LA.  He mentioned at the time, “Eliot, I've created a foolproof child protection scheme for the home.”  My response was, “Are you now or have you ever been a child?”  Precisely because kids are very persistent and determined to get around these things.
